L0_description: The jumper chose to exit in strong, sustained, gusting winds that were clearly observable and assessed as unjumpable by his companions—two of whom walked down and one explicitly warned him not to jump. The domain reference establishes that wind conditions frequently rule out safe jump plans and that no objects are good above 20 mph; horizontal flags indicate winds far exceeding this threshold. The fatal wind event was not unforeseeable; the decision to jump in known dangerous conditions constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Brett chose to jump in conditions that were clearly beyond safe limits. Winds were strong with sustained gusts, flags were horizontal, conditions had worsened over 45 minutes of waiting, and two companions abandoned the jump with one explicitly warning him it was not worth it. The decision to launch was driven by desperation to get a final jump before departing the next day. The wind overpowered his canopy's forward penetration and drove him back into the cliff, producing the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This self-imposed time pressure and urgency overrode his assessment of clearly dangerous conditions, the decision of two companions to walk down, and an explicit warning that it was not worth it. The desperation to capitalize on the last available opportunity drove the decision to launch in winds that were beyond safe limits.

L0_description: The bridle was misrouted around the right leg strap during packing, preventing canopy extraction and causing a total malfunction (PC in tow, container stayed closed). The narrative explicitly states the jumper packed in a rush and that neither the jumper nor his partner performed a gear check before exit, either of which would have caught the rigging error immediately.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing the evening before the jump, the jumper misrouted the bridle around the right leg strap instead of routing it cleanly from the container to the pilot chute. The jumper was rushing through the pack job to finish early. The misrouted bridle locked against the leg strap under load, preventing canopy extraction despite a fully inflated pilot chute, resulting in a total malfunction and closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was rushing through packing the evening before in order to go to sleep early and catch early morning conditions. This haste degraded the care and attention applied to the packing process, directly contributing to the bridle being misrouted around the right leg strap without detection.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his companion performed a gear check before exit. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately revealed the misrouting. This omitted preparatory step allowed the packing error from the previous evening to persist undetected through gearing up and exit, enabling the total malfunction.

L0_description: The jumper elected to fly new wingsuit proximity lines over a featureless snowfield in flat light conditions (heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture) that were observable before the jump. Flat light is a well-known hazard that eliminates depth cues essential for altitude awareness during terrain flying. The decision to initiate proximity flight in conditions that foreseeably precluded visual altitude reference constitutes a human error in judgment, as accepted practice requires adequate visual conditions along the entire flight path and the ability to maintain altitude awareness near terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly wingsuit proximity lines in conditions of heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture, and low-level clouds — complete flat light. These conditions were observable before the jump and predictably eliminated the depth cues essential for terrain-proximity wingsuit flight. The decision to proceed placed the jumper in a situation where visual altitude reference was effectively impossible.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Visual illusion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Flat light from featureless snow cover, absence of shadows, and low clouds eliminated all depth cues, causing the jumper to misperceive his altitude above the snowfield. Helmet camera footage confirms a relaxed body posture with no deployment initiation and no corrective input, consistent with a jumper who believed he had ample altitude. He flew into terrain at full speed without any indication he perceived the ground approaching.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper overestimated his ability to maintain altitude awareness during proximity wingsuit flight in complete flat light. His relaxed posture and absence of urgency throughout the flight indicate he was confident in his altitude perception despite conditions that predictably eliminated all visual depth cues required for terrain-proximity flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was on a dedicated expedition to Baffin Island to fly new wingsuit lines, representing significant investment in travel, logistics, and limited weather windows in a remote Arctic location. This expedition context plausibly contributed to the decision to proceed in flat-light conditions rather than waiting for adequate visibility, consistent with reluctance to forgo opportunities after major investment of effort.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture created complete flat light that caused the jumper's visual system to fail to correctly perceive his altitude and distance relative to the snowfield. The domain reference explicitly identifies flat light and snow cover as conditions producing spatial disorientation. The helmet camera confirms zero corrective input, consistent with a total failure of altitude perception.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate line familiarity
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper planned to fly new (previously unflown) wingsuit proximity lines, contrary to accepted practice requiring intimate knowledge of the entire line before flying it and maintaining distance on first flights. Flying unfamiliar lines compounded the risk of the flat-light decision by removing any mental reference points from prior flights that might have supplemented degraded visual depth cues.

L2_preconditions.label: Flat light from featureless snow cover
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow eliminating all shadows, contrast, and texture on the snowfield, combined with low-level clouds, actively degraded the jumper's visual perception during flight execution. These environmental conditions directly caused the altitude misperception coded at 103O by removing every available depth cue from the terrain surface.

L0_description: The narrative provides positive evidence that the fatality resulted from a manufacturing defect in the main lift web nylon webbing (incorrect heat treatment during production causing internal degradation). The manufacturer investigation confirmed the defective batch, and a recall was issued. The defect was not detectable without destructive testing, and the jumper's packing, rigging, maintenance, inspection, and execution were all explicitly confirmed as correct. This constitutes an equipment failure independent of human error.

The narrative provides positive evidence of an unforeseeable environmental event. The jumper executed all phases correctly—stable exit, good track, on-heading opening with good separation from the wall. A sudden localized rotor caused by a temperature inversion and differential heating between shaded and sun-exposed valley faces violently collapsed the canopy and turned him into the wall with no time to react. Meteorologists confirmed the thermal event could not have been predicted or detected from the exit point, and no wind indicators showed any movement before or after the jump.

The coroner confirmed a massive brainstem stroke caused by a congenital arterial malformation that had never produced symptoms, resulting in instant incapacitation and inability to deploy. The jumper had no prior medical history, a clean physical two months prior, and the condition was undetectable through normal medical evaluation. This constitutes a sudden medical event outside the jumper's reasonable ability to anticipate or control.

The narrative explicitly identifies two human errors: the jumper decided to jump in 20-knot sustained winds with gusts over 30 knots crosswind—conditions that four experienced companions judged unsafe and stood down from—and then failed to prioritize deploying his parachute over recovering stability during four seconds of tumbling along the cliff face. The wind conditions were fully observable before the jump and far exceeded accepted limits, making this a decision error, not an unforeseeable event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump in 20-knot sustained winds with gusts well over 30 knots from the side—conditions that four experienced companions assessed as unsafe and declined to jump in. The domain reference indicates no objects are good above 20 mph (~17 knots); these conditions far exceeded acceptable limits. This decision placed him in conditions where the crosswind gust immediately destabilized his exit and pushed him laterally back toward the wall, directly producing the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong procedure applied
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the 3–4 seconds of tumbling along the cliff face, the jumper failed to prioritize deploying his parachute over recovering stability, and never got a hand to his pilot chute before impact. Accepted practice establishes a clear priority order: (1) deploy, (2) deploy at the right altitude, (3) deploy in a stable body position—'whatever happens, pull.' By attempting to regain stability rather than immediately initiating deployment from any attitude, he forfeited the only remaining chance of canopy extraction before striking the ledge 80 meters below.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps and 3 seasons, the jumper assessed himself as capable of jumping in 20-knot sustained crosswinds with gusts well over 30 knots—conditions that four experienced companions independently evaluated as unsafe and declined. His self-assessment was clearly inconsistent with both the demands of the conditions and the consensus judgment of the group, indicating he unreasonably overestimated his capability relative to the specific task.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: After spending the entire morning at the exit watching conditions and watching all four companions stand down, the jumper chose to stay and wait another hour rather than walk away. His reluctance to abort after investing significant time and effort at the exit point, and his determination to jump despite unchanged dangerous conditions, indicates motivation driven by factors other than a sound assessment of readiness and conditions—consistent with sunk-cost commitment to the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: During the 3–4 seconds of tumbling along the cliff face, the jumper focused all conscious attention on recovering a stable body position to the exclusion of the higher-priority task of deploying his parachute from any attitude. This fixation on stability recovery prevented him from ever reaching his pilot chute, despite the accepted priority order requiring immediate deployment regardless of body position.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient emergency deployment training
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper lacked the ingrained muscle memory to immediately initiate deployment from a tumbling, head-low attitude near the cliff face. The accepted practice of deploying from any freefall attitude ('whatever happens, pull') requires trained automatic responses developed through extensive practice; his failure to prioritize deployment over stability recovery during the available 3–4 seconds suggests this critical emergency response was not sufficiently developed.

L0_description: Multiple human errors are explicitly described. The jumper decided to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption (vomiting, headache, visibly unsteady), directly contrary to accepted fitness-to-jump norms and despite warnings from companions. His impaired coordination caused his legs to buckle on push-off, producing an immediately unstable exit. Most critically, he then fixated on recovering stability rather than deploying his pilot chute, violating the fundamental priority hierarchy stated in accepted practice: deploy first, deploy at the right altitude, deploy stable—'Whatever happens, pull.'

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before, despite obvious signs of impairment (vomiting twice on the approach, splitting headache, visible unsteadiness) and explicit warnings from companions to sit out. This decision placed him in a state where his physical coordination was degraded, directly contributing to the failed exit and his inability to manage the resulting emergency.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During approximately 6 seconds of freefall after going unstable on exit, the jumper fixated entirely on recovering a belly-to-earth body position and failed to deploy his pilot chute, which remained in the BOC at impact. The accepted priority order for a poor exit is to deploy regardless of body position. He had sufficient time to pitch the PC even from an unstable attitude, but task fixation on stability recovery caused him to lose altitude awareness and never initiate deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before—vomiting twice on the approach, splitting headache, visibly unsteady. This hangover state degraded his judgment capacity, contributing to his decision to jump despite obvious signs of incapacity and explicit warnings from companions.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'the hangover affected his coordination,' which caused his legs to buckle on the exit push, producing the immediate instability that triggered the fixation on recovery. The degraded cognitive function from severe hangover also reduced his capacity to manage the emergency correctly by prioritizing deployment over stability recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Chris had jumped hung over before without issues, leading him to overestimate his ability to perform while severely impaired. His statement that he would 'be fine once the adrenaline kicked in' demonstrates a specific misjudgment of his capability relative to the demands of this jump, despite his condition being visibly worse than companions had seen before.

L2_preconditions.label: Normalization of deviance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'Chris had jumped hung over before without issues' and 'the decision to jump was not unusual for him,' indicating a sustained pattern of jumping while impaired that he had gradually accepted as his personal norm. This pattern went uncorrected despite being a known risk, removing the internal check that might have prevented the fatal decision to jump in a severely degraded state.

L0_description: Multiple human errors are clearly supported by the narrative. The jumper had only 2 skydives in the tracking suit versus the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, and had never jumped the suit from a cliff. He chose an intermediate-to-advanced cliff despite having only 40 BASE jumps from lower exits and declined advice to start with a more forgiving object. He was visibly exhausted after driving 14 hours through the night, directly degrading physical performance. His inability to generate forward drive in the unfamiliar suit resulted in insufficient separation and subterminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to BASE jump an intermediate-to-advanced cliff exit requiring mandatory tracking with a tracking suit in which he had completed only 2 skydives, far below the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before any BASE use. He rejected advice from a more experienced jumper to select a more forgiving exit, basing his confidence on video footage rather than personal proficiency. This decision placed him in a situation where his inability to fly the suit would be fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper could not achieve effective forward drive in the tracking suit after exit. The narrative explicitly states the suit was flying him rather than the other way around, and he fell at a steep angle without generating the horizontal separation needed to clear the terrain feature where the wall comes back in. He impacted a ledge at approximately 7 seconds, never having achieved adequate separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper unreasonably overestimated his ability to fly a tracking suit he had completed only 2 skydives in, basing his self-assessment on watching video footage rather than personal proficiency. He stated the intermediate-to-advanced exit 'looked easy from the footage' and rejected explicit advice from a more experienced jumper to select a more forgiving exit. His self-assessment was grossly inconsistent with his actual capability, directly enabling the decision to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. Acute sleep deprivation from overnight driving degrades judgment and decision-making capacity. This fatigued state impaired his ability to accurately assess risk and contributed to his decision to proceed despite clear indicators that the jump exceeded his capability, including rejecting sound advice from an experienced companion. The exhaustion also likely degraded the physical coordination needed to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience for BASE
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had completed only 2 skydives in the tracking suit versus the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before any BASE use. He had never jumped the suit from a cliff. Per accepted progression norms, he had not found the ideal body position for maximum forward speed and glide, did not have consistent openings in the suit, and could not transform an unstable position into a solid track. This directly caused his inability to generate forward drive after exit.

L2_preconditions.label: No slider-up cliff or tracking experience
L2_preconditions.description: All 40 of the jumper's BASE jumps were from lower exits. He had no experience with slider-up cliff objects requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking. The exit required tracking past a technical section where the wall comes back in — a skill set developed through progressive cliff experience and sub-terminal tracking training. His complete lack of cliff tracking experience meant he had no foundation for the performance demands of this jump.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading facing the building on a static line jump. Accepted practice for this specific scenario (static line, 180° off-heading facing the object) is to pull both rear risers to halt forward speed and land the stalled canopy at the object's base. Instead, she went for toggles, found only one, and pulled it hard — a single-brake input that created a diving turn directly into the building. This incorrect emergency response technique constitutes a human error that directly caused the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong procedure applied
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Facing a 180° off-heading directly into the building on a static line jump, the jumper went straight for her toggles instead of rear risers. The domain reference specifically prescribes pulling both rear risers and landing the stalled canopy at the object's base for a static line 180° off-heading pointed directly at the object. Rear risers would have arrested forward speed rather than producing a diving turn, and risers are a larger target area, reducing the risk of grabbing only one control input.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Attempting a heading correction with brakes, the jumper found only the right toggle and pulled it hard. A proper brake correction for a 180° requires grabbing both toggles simultaneously to arrest forward speed before releasing one side. The single hard toggle input created a diving turn that drove her directly into the building's glass facade, converting an off-heading into a fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient emergency off-heading correction training
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps, the jumper had not internalized the correct emergency procedure for a static line 180° off-heading pointed directly at the object. The domain reference explicitly prescribes rear risers for this scenario, yet she defaulted to toggles without hesitation, indicating this specific emergency response had not been trained to the point of automatic recall. The domain reference emphasizes that off-heading corrections must be practiced extensively at simple spans until reactions become natural and instant.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient emergency off-heading correction training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's inability to locate both toggles and execute a coordinated brake correction indicates insufficient muscle memory for emergency heading corrections. With 90 BASE jumps, she had not developed the practiced, automatic fine-motor skill needed to reliably grab both toggles under time pressure. The domain reference notes that toggles should be big, bright, and stiffened for easy grabbing, and that corrections must be practiced until the reaction is natural and instant.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: A static line 180° off-heading directly into a building facade provided an extremely compressed time window of approximately 1-3 seconds in which to assess heading, select a procedure, and execute it. The cognitive and motor demands of this emergency exceeded the jumper's processing capacity, contributing to her failure to locate both toggles and resulting in the uncontrolled single-toggle diving turn into the building.

L0_description: The jumper chose to exit a cliff in near-zero visibility fog conditions that were clearly observable at the exit point, while all other jumpers recognized the conditions as unjumpable and packed up to hike down. Jumping in zero visibility from a cliff with terrain features (protruding ledges) that cannot be seen violates accepted practice for weather evaluation and object assessment. The decision to jump despite being unable to see the wall, landing area, or flight path constitutes a clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to exit a terminal cliff in essentially zero visibility conditions (fog obscuring the wall, fjord, and landing area) when all other jumpers recognized the conditions were unsafe and chose to hike down. He believed his familiarity with the exit would allow him to navigate by feel and track on the correct bearing to clear terrain unseen. This decision placed him in freefall with no ability to see terrain features, directly producing the fatal impact with a protruding ledge approximately 120 meters below the exit that he could not have seen. The pilot chute remained stowed, indicating he struck the ledge before reaching his planned deployment point.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly stated he had done this exit dozens of times and knew the heading by feel, believing his extensive site familiarity would compensate for zero visibility. This overestimation of his capability to navigate without visual references directly contributed to his decision to exit when all other jumpers recognized conditions were unsafe and chose to hike down. His self-assessment was clearly inconsistent with the actual demands of the task, as no amount of familiarity enables a jumper to detect an unseen protruding ledge in fog.

L0_description: The jumper interrupted his tracking flight to reach up and adjust a helmet-mounted GoPro, directly contradicting accepted practice that a camera should be forgotten by jump time. This action collapsed his tracking body position, eliminated object separation, and caused him to drift into the wall. Despite the ledge being visible for several seconds, he made no corrective input and never deployed, constituting a failure to prioritize deployment and maintain situational awareness.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during active tracking flight near a cliff face. This deliberate action disrupted his tracking body position, causing him to go flat and drift laterally toward the wall. Adjusting a camera mid-flight while tracking near terrain was an inappropriate prioritization that directly produced the loss of separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While focused on the camera adjustment, the jumper failed to monitor terrain proximity and made no corrective input despite a ledge being in clear view for several seconds before impact. He neither resumed tracking, attempted to turn away from the wall, nor deployed his parachute. His attention was captured by the camera task to the exclusion of the critical tasks of object separation and deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Once the jumper began adjusting the helmet-mounted GoPro, his attention became fixated on the camera task to the exclusion of terrain proximity, body position, and object separation. This cognitive narrowing is what prevented him from perceiving the approaching ledge or making any corrective input despite it being in clear view for several seconds, directly enabling the L1 attention failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's designated role as camera person on the two-way created a motivation to ensure the filming was successful. This misplaced prioritization of the filming objective over flight safety drove the decision to reach up and adjust the GoPro during active tracking flight near terrain, rather than abandoning the filming task and maintaining tracking discipline.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate pre-jump camera setup
L2_preconditions.description: Accepted practice requires configuring the camera before exit and forgetting about it during the jump. The fact that the jumper felt compelled to adjust the camera mid-flight indicates the camera was not adequately secured or verified before exit. Proper pre-jump camera preparation would have eliminated the perceived need for an in-flight adjustment, removing the trigger for the L1 decision error.

L0_description: The jumper experienced repeated near-miss stalls from poor angle of attack control, explicitly acknowledged the need for remedial skydiving training, yet continued jumping cliffs without ever completing that training. The fatal stall and terrain impact resulted directly from a known, unaddressed skill deficiency and the conscious decision to keep exposing himself to conditions requiring a skill he had not practiced, both clear human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued flying wingsuit BASE from cliffs every weekend despite a known, self-acknowledged deficiency in angle of attack control that had produced at least four near-miss stalls in the preceding three months. He explicitly identified that he needed to return to the dropzone for approximately 50 skydives of dedicated stall-recovery training before further cliff flights, yet never acted on this self-assessment. This decision to repeatedly expose himself to proximity terrain with an unresolved, clearly dangerous skill gap directly placed him in the fatal situation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Approximately 15 seconds into the flight, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to increase beyond sustainable flight parameters, causing the wingsuit to stall. His glide path transitioned from shallow to nearly vertical. This was the same AoA-control failure pattern he had experienced on at least four prior occasions, confirming a persistent technique execution deficit rather than an isolated event. The stall eliminated his glide and available lift, converting controlled flight into a terminal-velocity descent into terrain with no altitude remaining for recovery or deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls from the same AoA-control failure, explicitly identified the corrective action needed (approximately 50 dedicated stall-recovery skydives), communicated this assessment to peers, yet never followed through. He continued jumping cliffs every weekend for three months without addressing the deficiency. This sustained failure to act on a known, self-acknowledged weakness—including normalization of repeated near-misses—enabled the repeated decisions to expose himself to proximity terrain with an unresolved critical skill gap.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: By never returning to the dropzone to practice stall recovery as he himself identified was necessary, the jumper left his AoA-control technique unimproved across three months and multiple additional cliff flights. This sustained inaction on the known technical deficiency directly preserved the conditions under which the fatal stall occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's motivation to continue cliff jumping every weekend was disconnected from his own readiness assessment. He explicitly acknowledged he was not ready for further cliff flights without additional training, yet the desire to keep jumping overrode this self-assessment. His continued participation was driven by the pull of the activity rather than a sound evaluation of his preparedness.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate AoA control skill for proximity flight
L2_preconditions.description: Despite meeting general progression minimums (900 skydives, 300 WS skydives, 150 BASE jumps, 80 WS BASE jumps), the jumper's specific angle-of-attack management skill was demonstrably inadequate for proximity wingsuit flight near terrain. Four prior near-miss stalls from the identical failure pattern confirmed a persistent technique deficit, and the fatal stall at 15 seconds into flight was the same unresolved failure mode. His actual capability was insufficient for the demands of the task he attempted.

L0_description: Multiple human errors are deducible. The jumper conducted a night jump on an antenna with only two prior antenna jumps, both from more forgiving structures, violating the accepted practice of jumping an object in daytime first and progressing through familiar objects well within capability. Critically, upon experiencing a 90-degree off-heading toward guy wires approximately 40 feet away, the jumper made no heading correction attempt whatsoever—no riser input, no toggle input—indicating a freeze response consistent with encountering a situation beyond trained capability, representing a failure in skill-based performance and prior development of the object avoidance and heading correction skills that the domain reference identifies as essential and that must be practiced extensively at forgiving objects before progressing to more demanding ones.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump a 900-foot antenna at night with only 2 prior antenna jumps, both from taller, more forgiving structures with guy wires further from the exit point. Accepted practice requires night jumps to be at objects well within the jumper's capability with prior daytime familiarity, and antennas provide only ~120° of safe heading range making them technically demanding. This decision placed him on an unfamiliar, restricted-heading object in reduced-visibility conditions that exceeded his antenna experience.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading pointing directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away, the jumper made absolutely no corrective input. Multiple witnesses confirmed no toggle input, no riser input, and arms remaining at his sides. Accepted practice requires immediate rear riser or brake input to correct heading, with a 180-degree riser turn achievable in approximately one second. The complete freeze response, consistent with unpracticed emergency reactions and lack of drilled muscle memory for heading corrections, was the direct mechanism of the fatal wire strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: With only 60 BASE jumps and 2 prior antenna jumps—both from taller, more forgiving structures with guy wires further from the exit point—the jumper overestimated his capability to safely jump a more demanding antenna at night. His self-assessment was inconsistent with his actual antenna experience and the restricted heading range (~120°) this object demanded.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses confirmed the jumper completely froze upon seeing guy wires directly ahead after the 90-degree off-heading opening. This automatic physiological freeze response—triggered by the sudden, unexpected proximity of the wires—rendered him unable to initiate any corrective riser or toggle input during the approximately two seconds available before impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to adequately evaluate how the closer guy wire proximity on this 900-foot antenna compared to his two previous antenna jumps from taller structures with wires further from the exit. Additionally, accepted practice requires daytime familiarity with an object before attempting a night jump; there is no evidence he had jumped this antenna in daylight, meaning he lacked knowledge of exit-to-wire distances and heading constraints.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient antenna experience and unpracticed heading corrections
L2_preconditions.description: With only 60 BASE jumps, 2 antenna jumps from more forgiving structures, and no evidence of extensively drilled off-heading correction practice, the jumper lacked the muscle memory required for instant riser or brake input on a restricted-heading object. Accepted practice requires developing heading correction skills through repeated practice at simple spans before progressing to objects where off-headings are immediately life-threatening. This training deficit made the freeze response far more likely and more consequential.

L0_description: The jumper knowingly used a pilot chute with a large tear sustained on the previous jump, despite being warned it needed replacement and being offered spare PCs by other jumpers. The damaged PC failed to inflate, resulting in a total malfunction and closed-container impact. This constitutes a human error in equipment maintenance and the decision to jump with known-damaged critical equipment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump with a pilot chute he knew was severely damaged — a ~4-inch tear in the mesh from the previous jump. He was explicitly told the PC needed to be replaced, and multiple people offered to lend him a spare. He examined the damage, dismissed the risk, and jumped anyway. This conscious decision to use critically damaged equipment was the direct producing mechanism of the total malfunction: the torn PC could not hold air, collapsed in his burble, and he impacted with a closed container.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper unreasonably overestimated the capability of his damaged equipment. After examining a ~4-inch tear in the PC mesh, being explicitly told by at least one person that it needed replacement, and being offered spare PCs by multiple jumpers, he concluded it would 'still catch enough air.' This misjudgment of equipment capability directly enabled the decision to jump with critically damaged gear.

L0_description: Multiple human errors are explicitly described: the group failed to establish a proper separation plan, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for a multi-way jump, violating accepted multi-way planning norms. The deceased tracked directly over the second jumper and deployed his pilot chute into that jumper's inflating canopy without checking his airspace, resulting in a fatal canopy entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David participated in a 3-way multi-way jump without establishing a proper separation plan, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. The domain reference for multi-way jumps explicitly requires choosing and rehearsing exit order, staged openings with vertical and horizontal separation, and pre-planned flight paths. Proceeding with the jump absent these critical safeguards placed David in a foreseeable collision scenario.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David tracked directly over the second jumper and deployed his pilot chute without checking his airspace, pitching directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy. The narrative explicitly states David did not check his airspace before deploying. This failure to maintain situational awareness of other jumpers' positions during both the tracking phase and the deployment moment directly produced the fatal canopy entanglement.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate briefing in group jump
L2_preconditions.description: The group failed to establish specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for the 3-way jump. Without a pre-established separation framework, David had no mental model of where the second jumper would be during tracking and deployment, directly contributing to his failure to check airspace before pitching.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The group's casual approach to the multi-way — simply stating exit order with no further coordination — reflects a complacent attitude toward multi-way procedures that carried into execution. This reduced alertness contributed to David's failure to monitor other jumpers' positions during tracking and to check his airspace before deploying.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: With 120 BASE jumps across 3 seasons, David proceeded with a 3-way cliff jump despite knowing no specific separation plan, delays, or flight paths had been established. This indicates he overestimated the group's collective ability to safely manage a multi-way jump without formal coordination, a task the domain reference identifies as exponentially more risky and requiring thorough pre-planning.

L0_description: The jumper deliberately deployed his pilot chute at an altitude too low for the canopy to fully open, impacting while the canopy was still extracting. The narrative explicitly states he had a pattern of progressively pushing delays longer despite repeated warnings from other jumpers, and that there were no equipment, weather, or exit issues — he simply chose to pull too low.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately chose to deploy at a dangerously low altitude, continuing a documented pattern of progressively extending delays from 4 seconds to 6, 8, and beyond over the preceding season. The narrative explicitly states he chose to pull low and that multiple other jumpers had warned him about this behavior. On this jump the delay exceeded the available altitude, and the canopy was still extracting from the deployment bag at impact. No equipment, weather, or exit instability factors were present; the fatal outcome was produced entirely by the decision to delay deployment beyond the altitude required for canopy inflation.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper progressively extended his delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds over the preceding season despite multiple warnings from other jumpers, indicating he unreasonably overestimated his ability to judge minimum safe deployment altitude. Each successful low pull reinforced his belief that he could continue pushing further, creating an inflated self-assessment inconsistent with the actual margins available.

L2_preconditions.label: Normalization of deviance
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was explicitly aware of the increasing risk through multiple direct warnings from other jumpers over the preceding season, yet failed to take corrective action. He gradually accepted progressively lower pulls as his personal standard, with delays escalating from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds, representing a sustained pattern of inaction in response to known feedback about a dangerous trend.

L0_description: Multiple human errors are deducible. The jumper upgraded to an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only ~10 skydives in that specific suit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific wingsuit skydives before BASE use, and had only 40 total WS BASE jumps—insufficient to have fully utilized an entry-level suit before upgrading. During flight he stalled the suit, a recognized fatal error pattern of flying at too-high an angle of attack, losing speed and available lift with no altitude to recover. The narrative explicitly states he had not put in the required time to understand the expert suit's stall boundaries.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to BASE jump an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives in the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. The domain reference explicitly states that upgrading wingsuits before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error, and that even an entry-level suit requires 30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential before considering an upgrade. This decision placed the jumper in a flight regime whose stall boundaries he did not understand, directly producing the conditions that killed him.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the second half of the flight, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to shift into a stall, causing his glide to transition from shallow to nearly vertical over approximately two seconds. He was unable to recover from the developing stall and continued sinking until terrain impact. This in-flight execution failure—failing to maintain adequate airspeed and proper AoA management, and failing to recover from the stall—was the immediate mechanism of the fatal impact, driven by insufficient familiarity with the expert suit's narrow margin between efficient flight and deep stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper unreasonably overestimated his ability to handle an expert-level wingsuit in BASE based on his intermediate-suit experience. With only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1—far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific skydives—he assessed himself as ready for wingsuit BASE with a suit whose stall boundaries he had not learned. This overestimation of capability directly drove the decision to jump the expert suit off a cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient training in specific wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific skydives before wingsuit BASE jumping. The domain reference states that even an entry-level suit requires 30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential, and that upgrading before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error. His insufficient familiarity with the expert suit's narrow margin between efficient flight and deep stall meant he lacked the skill and muscle memory to maintain proper AoA and recover when the suit entered a stall regime, directly producing the fatal technique failure.

L0_description: The jumper had only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, far below the accepted minimum of 200-250 skydives before beginning BASE. Jumping a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking with a tracking suit—having never completed the accepted progression through spans or developed any BASE skills—represents a profound deviation from accepted practice. The unstable exit and/or insufficient object separation leading to impact are direct consequences of attempting a highly technical jump without the requisite experience.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up cliff BASE jump wearing a tracking suit with only 5 total skydives and zero BASE jumps. Accepted practice requires a minimum of 200+ skydives, mentored progression from simple objects (spans), and 40-50 tracking suit skydives before any tracking suit BASE use. Jumping a terminal/sub-terminal cliff as a first-ever BASE jump with virtually no skydiving experience represents a catastrophic departure from accepted progression norms and directly placed the jumper in a situation they could not survive.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an unstable exit and failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff face during sub-terminal freefall. With only 5 skydives and no BASE experience, the jumper lacked the fundamental body position, tracking, and exit skills required for cliff jumping. The resulting insufficient object separation was the direct physical mechanism producing the fatal cliff-impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient skydiving and BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 total skydives, zero BASE jumps, and zero tracking suit experience in skydiving. Accepted practice requires 200+ skydives, an FJC, mentored progression beginning at simple spans, and 40-50 tracking suit skydives before tracking suit BASE use. This extreme experience deficit left the jumper unable to assess the risk of a slider-up cliff jump, directly contributing to the catastrophically poor judgment to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient skydiving and BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, the jumper had none of the foundational skills required for cliff jumping: no exit technique, no sub-terminal tracking ability, no body position control at deployment, and no object avoidance proficiency. This total absence of trained skill directly produced the unstable exit and failure to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Attempting a slider-up cliff jump with a tracking suit while possessing only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps indicates the jumper grossly overestimated their capability relative to the demands of the task. The gap between the jumper's actual ability and the minimum competence required for this jump type is so extreme that proceeding reflects an unreasonable self-assessment of capability, contributing directly to the decision to jump.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states a fatal packing error: the jumper packed the pilot chute inside the main container beneath the closing flaps instead of routing it to the BOC pouch. When the BOC handle was pulled, the PC remained trapped inside the container, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow) with no canopy extraction. Additionally, the jumper decided to jump in deteriorating weather with increasing winds and rain despite visible hesitation, which constitutes a separate decision error even though it was not the proximate cause of death.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Memory failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper placed the pilot chute inside the main container on top of the folded canopy instead of routing it to the BOC pouch. When the BOC handle was pulled in freefall it extracted cleanly, but no pilot chute was connected to the deployment system—the PC remained trapped beneath the closing flaps inside the sealed container. This produced a total malfunction with no possibility of canopy extraction, resulting in fatal ground impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: Placing the pilot chute inside the main container instead of routing it to the BOC pouch is a fundamental step omission in a well-practiced routine procedure. With 90 BASE jumps and 500 skydives over three seasons, the jumper had performed this packing sequence many times. The nature of the error—omitting a critical procedural step in a familiar, repetitive task—is characteristic of complacency or inattention during routine performance, where familiarity reduced the alertness needed to catch a gross sequencing mistake.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check verifying the deployment system—confirming the pilot chute was properly seated in the BOC pouch and the bridle was correctly routed from PC through the closing pins—would have revealed that the PC was missing from the BOC pouch and trapped inside the container. The error was discovered only by the rescue team after the accident, indicating that no adequate gear check was performed or that the check failed to trace the critical PC-to-BOC routing before exit.

L0_description: The report contains no accident narrative and no structured data beyond date, location, and object type (Earth). There is no information from which to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause.

The narrative states only that the jumper impacted talus below the exit with the canopy out and that there were no witnesses. No information is provided regarding experience level, equipment configuration, packing, weather conditions, or the state of the canopy at impact. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify positive evidence of a non-human cause.

Two experienced witnesses provide contradictory accounts—one describing a late pull, the other describing a PC in tow after an early deployment—and the gear was removed by rescue crews before inspection, making reconciliation impossible. The narrative explicitly states there is no way to determine what occurred without the equipment. Neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be established with sufficient confidence.

The jumper never attempted deployment despite maintaining an active, controlled body position throughout freefall. Comprehensive post-mortem investigation (autopsy, toxicology, equipment inspection) ruled out medical events, substance impairment, and equipment malfunction. The narrative explicitly states no cause was identified, providing neither sufficient basis to deduce a human error pathway nor positive evidence of a non-human cause.

The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall during a wingsuit flight with proximity flying listed as a possible factor, but the narrative contains no technical details about the flight or the circumstances leading to the impact. The reporting organization explicitly states they are seeking more information about the jump. Without any evidence of what occurred during the flight, neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be established.

The narrative explicitly states that no one witnessed the jump, no one inspected the gear before authorities removed it, and all proposed explanations (off-heading, packing issue, thermals) are characterized as speculation with no evidentiary basis. While the canopy was out and object strike under canopy is established, the cause of the object strike cannot be determined from the available information. Neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be supported.

The narrative explicitly states it is impossible to determine what happened. There are no witnesses, no known date of the jump, no known weather conditions at the time, and no physical evidence describing the failure mode. While the cliff is known for unpredictable crosswinds and strong winds are listed as a possible factor, this is speculative—there is insufficient information to support either a human error pathway or positive evidence of a non-human cause.

The narrative provides virtually no information about the jumper, their experience, equipment, packing, weather conditions, or the sequence of events. The only known facts are that a parachute was seen descending near Trollveggen and a body was recovered by helicopter. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a positive non-human cause.

While it is clear the jumper deployed too late—impacting at line stretch—the narrative explicitly states the reason cannot be determined and presents three competing explanations (sun glare impairing altitude judgment, tracking suit flying steeper than expected, task fixation on landing area). No video exists, and the narrative concludes 'Without footage there is no way to determine whether this was a visual issue, a suit performance issue, or an attention issue.' Because the specific causal pathway cannot be distinguished among these alternatives, and the narrative affirmatively declares the cause unknowable, a confident human error classification cannot be made without speculation beyond the provided information.

The cause of death is explicitly listed as 'Malfunction, Misrigging,' and the narrative explicitly states 'it is not a manufacturer error.' Misrigging is a human error in the jumper's packing or rigging of equipment—an area where the domain reference establishes that in BASE, the jumper alone is responsible for equipment assembly and maintenance. The malfunction that the jumper could not resolve was a direct consequence of this rigging error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper misrigged their equipment, which produced a malfunction that could not be resolved before impact. The narrative and structured fields confirm the cause of death as misrigging and explicitly rule out manufacturer error, indicating the jumper made an error in equipment assembly or configuration. The exact nature of the misrigging is not specified, but it was the producing mechanism of the fatal malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform adequate pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper exited with misrigged equipment, indicating that the pre-jump gear check was either omitted or insufficiently thorough. A proper gear inspection — tracing the bridle, verifying all connections, slider routing, and line configuration — should have identified the rigging error before jumping. This preparatory omission allowed the L1 misrigging error to persist undetected into the jump.
